chunks.text: Stereo. HC JD A 38. Judgment Sheet IN THE LAHORE HIGH COURT, BAHAWALPUR BENCH, BAHAWALPUR. JUDICIAL DEPARTMENT Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 (The State Vs. Muhammad Ismail) Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 (Muhammad Ismail Vs. The State and another) J U D G M E N T Date of hearing: 28.10.2021. Appellant by: Ms. Tania Bazai, Advocate. (Learned defence counsel appointed at State expense). State by: Ch. Asghar Ali Gill, Deputy Prosecutor General. SADIQ MAHMUD KHURRAM, J. –Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din (convict) was tried by the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Chishtian in case FIR No. 50 of 2018 dated 23.01.2018 registered at Police Station City Chistian District Bahawalnagar in respect of an offence under section 302 PPC for committing the Qatl-i-Amd of Gulzar Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased). The learned trial court vide judgment dated 28.01.2019, convicted Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din (convict) and sentenced him as infra: Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din: Death under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Gulzar Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.200,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 2 heirs of the deceased and in case of default thereof, the convict was directed to further undergo six months of simple imprisonment. The convict was ordered to be hanged by his neck till dead. 2. Feeling aggrieved, Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din (convict) lodged Criminal Appeal No.53 of 2019 assailing his conviction and sentence. The learned trial court submitted Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 under section 374 Cr.P.C. seeking confirmation or otherwise of the sentence of death awarded to the appellant namely Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din.

Feeling aggrieved, Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din (convict) lodged Criminal Appeal No.53 of 2019 assailing his conviction and sentence. The learned trial court submitted Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 under section 374 Cr.P.C. seeking confirmation or otherwise of the sentence of death awarded to the appellant namely Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din. We intend to decide the Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 and Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 through this single judgment. 3. Precisely, the necessary facts of the prosecution case, as stated by Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), the complainant of the case, are as under:- “Stated that 1 run a business of selling cloth on my own motorcycle from village to village on daily basis. My brother Gulzar Ahmed deceased also runs the same business. My brother Gulzar Ahmed resides in Bhatta Colony Chishtian in a rented house. On 23 01-2018 at about 06:00PM Muhammad Tariq s/o Muhammad Ismaeel caste Ansari came to my house and inquired about Gulzar Ahmed and he told me that he had to purchase cloth from Faisalabad through Gulzar Ahmed and he wants to meet him to give him some money for purchasing of the cloth from Faisalabad. I replied to Muhammad Tariq that usually he returns back and visits my house before Maghrab but today he did not come in my house. On 23-01-2018 at about 06:30 PM I alongwith Tariq and my son Dastageer reached at the house of Gulzar Ahmed to give him some money for purchasing cloth. When we entered in his house there was hue and cry in his residential room. We saw that Muhammad Ismaeel accused armed with Churi was giving Churi blows on body of Gulzar Ahmed. Before our eyesight. Muhammad Ismaeel accused gave 6/7 Churi Blows to Gulzar Ahmed at his chest, hands, neck and back. We raised noise and tried to apprehend him but he fled away from the scene by brandishing Churi. My brother Gulzar Ahmad succumbed to injuries. On telephonic call of Muhammad Tariq PW, police arrived there and we told the whole story to the police.

My brother Gulzar Ahmad succumbed to injuries. On telephonic call of Muhammad Tariq PW, police arrived there and we told the whole story to the police. Muhammad Akram SI recorded my statement Exh-PA, read the same over me and obtained my signature on it. Motive behind the occurrence is that accused Muhammad Ismaeel demanded money as loan from my brother Gulzar Ahmed but he refused to give the same. On the fateful day this Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 3 fact was in the knowledge of accused Muhammad Ismaeel that there is huge amount in the pocket of Gulzar Ahmed and he has to go Faisalabad for purchasing cloth. Accused came there and tried to snatch money but on his resistance, he murdered him with Churi. The accused murdered my brother Gulzar Ahmed without any cause, he should be convicted strictly. On the same day I received dead body of my brother Gulzar Ahmed deceased. The IO prepared receipt. Exh-PB which bears my signature.” 4. After the formal investigation of the case report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court wherein the appellant namely Muhammad Ismaeel son of Shahab Din was sent to face trial. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 09.03.2018, to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5. The prosecution, in order to prove its case, got statements of as many as eleven witnesses recorded. The ocular account of the case was furnished by Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3). Muhammad Akram, SI (PW-4) submitted that on 23.01.2018, he reached the place of occurrence and recorded the oral statement (Exh.PA) of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1). Muhammad Riaz, ASI (PW-5) stated that on 23.01.2018 he recorded the formal F.I.R (Exh.PA/1).

Muhammad Akram, SI (PW-4) submitted that on 23.01.2018, he reached the place of occurrence and recorded the oral statement (Exh.PA) of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1). Muhammad Riaz, ASI (PW-5) stated that on 23.01.2018 he recorded the formal F.I.R (Exh.PA/1). Mehboob Manzoor 1123/C (PW-6) stated that on 23.01.2018 he escorted the dead body of the deceased to the hospital for its post mortem examination and also received the last worn clothes of the deceased from the Medical Officer. Muhammad Jamil, Draftsman, (PW-7) stated that he prepared the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PG). Shaukat Ali 1002/HC (PW-8) stated that on 23.01.2018 the Investigating Officer of the case Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 4 handed over to him four sealed parcels which on 30.01.2018 he handed over the same to Khalid Rauf, SI (PW-9) for their onward transmission to the office of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore. Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, investigated the case from 23.01.2018 till 07.01.2019, arrested the appellant on 05.02.2018, and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. 6. The prosecution also got Dr. Muhammad Waheed Amjad (PW-10) examined, who on 23.01.2018 was posted as Medical Officer at THQ hospital Chishtian and on the same day conducted the postmortem examination of the dead body of Gulzar Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased). Dr.,Muhammad Waheed Amjad (PW-10) on examining the dead body of Gulzar Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased) observed as under:- “ Description of injuries. Injury No.1. An incised wound measuring 3 cm x 2 cm x muscle deep present on right side of the neck near thyroid cartilage. Under lying vessels damaged. Injury No.2. An incised wound measuring 3 x 2 cm x muscle deep present on the interior side of the neck.

Injury No.2. An incised wound measuring 3 x 2 cm x muscle deep present on the interior side of the neck. Injury No.3. An incised wound measuring 3 x 1 cm x muscle deep present on right side of upper chest. Injury No.4. Two incised wound measuring 3 x 1 cm x muscle deep and on dissection going into thorasic cavity damage right ventrical of the heart, present on left side of the chest. INJURY NO. 5. Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 5 An inside (sic) wound measuring 2 x 1 cm x skin deep. Present on interior side of the right arm. INJURY NO. 6. An incised wound measuring 4 x 1 cm x muscle deep present in first web space of right hand. INJURY NO. 7 An incised wound measuring 4 x 2 cm x muscle deep present on first web space of left hand. INJURY NO. 8. An incised wound measuring 4 x 1 cm x muscle deep present on posterior side of lower chest. On deep dissection damaging liver. INJURY NO. 9. An incised wound measuring 3x 2cm x muscle deep present on left side of neck damaging deep vessels of neck. Thyroid, arytenoids, hyoid bone intact.............................. FINAL OPINION. After performing external and internal examination of dead body, it can be said that injury No. 1,4 and 9 are sufficient to cause death..” 7. On 17.01.2019, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor closed the prosecution evidence after tendering in evidence the reports of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.PK) and the certificate of identification of the accused (Exh.PL). 8. After the closure of prosecution evidence, the learned trial court examined the appellant namely Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din under section 342 Cr.P.C. and in answer to the question why this case against you and why the PWs have deposed against you, he replied that he had been involved in the case falsely and was innocent.

On 17.01.2019, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor closed the prosecution evidence after tendering in evidence the reports of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.PK) and the certificate of identification of the accused (Exh.PL). 8. After the closure of prosecution evidence, the learned trial court examined the appellant namely Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din under section 342 Cr.P.C. and in answer to the question why this case against you and why the PWs have deposed against you, he replied that he had been involved in the case falsely and was innocent. The appellant namely Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din opted not to get himself examined Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 6 under section 340 Cr.P.C. and did not adduce any evidence in his defence. 9. On the conclusion of the trial, the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Chishtian convicted and sentenced the appellant as referred to above. 10. The primary contention of the learned counsel for the appellant is that the whole case is fabricated and false. The learned counsel for the appellant argued that the prosecution remained unable to prove the facts in issue and did not produce any unimpeachable, admissible and relevant evidence to prove the same. The learned counsel for the appellant further contended that the statements of the eye witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) were not worthy of any reliance. The learned counsel for the appellant also argued that the recoveries were full of procedural defects, of no legal worth and value and result of fake proceedings. The learned counsel for the appellant finally submitted that the prosecution has totally failed to prove the case against the appellant beyond the shadow of doubt. 11. On the other hand, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General contended that the prosecution had proved its case beyond shadow of doubt by producing independent witnesses. He further argued that the deceased died as a result of injuries suffered at the hands of the appellant. He further contended that the medical evidence also corroborated the statements of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam

He further argued that the deceased died as a result of injuries suffered at the hands of the appellant. He further contended that the medical evidence also corroborated the statements of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 7 Dastageer (PW-3). He further argued that the recovery of the Churri (P-4) from the appellant namely Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din also corroborated the ocular account. He further contended that there was no occasion for the prosecution witnesses, who were related to the deceased, to substitute the real offender with the innocent in this case. Lastly, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General prayed for the rejection of appeal as lodged by the appellant namely Muhammad Ismail son of Shahab Din. 12. We have heard the learned counsel for the appellant, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General and with their assistance perused the record and evidence recorded during the trial. 13. The whole prosecution case revolves around the statements of the eye witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3). The relationship of the said witnesses with each other and the deceased is on record. Gulzar Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali, deceased, was the brother of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) and brother in- law of Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and the paternal uncle of Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3). According to the prosecution witnesses the occurrence had taken place in a house rented by the deceased namely Gulzar Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali. It has been admitted by Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) that the said house was at a distance from the place of his residence and it took the witnesses ten minutes on a motorcycle to arrive from the house of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) to the house taken on rent by Gulzar Ahmad (deceased), on the Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 8 day of occurrence.

According to the prosecution witnesses the occurrence had taken place in a house rented by the deceased namely Gulzar Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali. It has been admitted by Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) that the said house was at a distance from the place of his residence and it took the witnesses ten minutes on a motorcycle to arrive from the house of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) to the house taken on rent by Gulzar Ahmad (deceased), on the Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 8 day of occurrence. Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) during cross-examination stated as under:- “ Tariq PW is my brother in law (Behnoi) and PW Ghulam Dastageer is my real son. The distance between residence of my brother (deceased) and PW Tariq is about one mile. It is incorrect to suggest that the said distance is about four kilometer. The distance between my residence and residence of deceased is approximately 03/04-acres........................ Tariq PW came on motorcycle to my house at about 06:00PM on the day of occurrence and he stayed at my house for about 5/7 minutes then we proceeded to the house of deceased. Motorcycle was driven by Tariq on which I, Tariq PW and Ghulam Dastageer were riding towards the house of deceased on the same motorcycle. It would have consumed about 10 minutes in reaching the house of deceased.” (emphasis supplied). The other witness namely Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) also admitted that he was not the resident of any place around the place of occurrence rather his house was at a distance of 2.7 kilometres from the rented house of the deceased Gulzar Ahmad, the house where the occurrence took place. Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) during cross-examination admitted as under:- “Gulzar was my brother in law. Distance between my house and house of complainant is about 2-5 KM. Distance between my house and the house of deceased Gulzar is about. 2- 3⁄4 -KM (Ponay 3-KM).”(emphasis supplied). Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 9 Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case also admitted during the cross-examination that none of the witnesses whose statements he had recorded during the investigation of the case were residents of the area around the place of occurrence.

Distance between my house and the house of deceased Gulzar is about. 2- 3⁄4 -KM (Ponay 3-KM).”(emphasis supplied). Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 9 Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case also admitted during the cross-examination that none of the witnesses whose statements he had recorded during the investigation of the case were residents of the area around the place of occurrence. Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11),the Investigating Officer of the case, admitted during the cross- examination as under:- “ It is correct that PWs and complainant are not residents of the area of place of occurrence.” We have also perused the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PG) as prepared by Muhammad Jamil, Draftsman (PW-7) and the rough site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PP) as prepared by Muhammad Akbar Inspector (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, and find that that the places of the residences of none of the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) nor the places of work or any shop of the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) are marked in the same. In view of the above mentioned facts, it can be validly held that all the three prosecution witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) were “chance witnesses” and therefore were under a duty to explain and prove their presence at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence. Strangely enough all the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) did not even offer any explanation as to why they found it necessary Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 10 to go to the house of the deceased at the end of the day when there did not exist any reason for them to do so. Furthermore, it was claimed by Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) that all the three witnesses had gone to the rented house of the deceased namely Gulzar Ahmad while riding on a motorcycle, however admittedly the said motorcycle used by the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) to arrive at the place of occurrence was neither taken into possession by the Investigating Officers of the case nor was produced by any of the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) during the investigation of the case.

Strangely enough all the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) did not even offer any explanation as to why they found it necessary Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 10 to go to the house of the deceased at the end of the day when there did not exist any reason for them to do so. Furthermore, it was claimed by Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) that all the three witnesses had gone to the rented house of the deceased namely Gulzar Ahmad while riding on a motorcycle, however admittedly the said motorcycle used by the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) to arrive at the place of occurrence was neither taken into possession by the Investigating Officers of the case nor was produced by any of the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) during the investigation of the case. The oral statement (Exh.PA) of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) was recorded by Muhammad Akram, SI (PW-4) when the said police officer arrived at the place of occurrence, however the motorcycle used by the witnesses to arrive at the place of occurrence was not observed to be present at the place of occurrence by Muhammad Akram, SI (PW-4) at the time of his arrival at the place of occurrence. Subsequently as mentioned above, the investigation of the case was conducted by Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11), who arrived at the place of occurrence on the same night, but he also did not observe the presence of any motorcycle used by the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3), at the time of his arrival and stay at the place of occurrence. The failure of the prosecution witnesses to prove that they had used any motorcycle to arrive at the place of occurrence has repercussions which point towards the fact that the claim of the prosecution witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 11 Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) in this regard was false. All these omissions are conspicuous by their absence. In absence of physical proof or the reason for the presence of the witnesses at the crime scene, the same cannot be relied upon. In this respect, reliance is placed on the case of “Muhammad Rafiq v. State”(2014 SCMR 1698) wherein the august Supreme Court of Pakistan rejected the claim of witnesses who lived one kilometre away from the occurrence, but on the day of occurrence stated to be present near the spot as they working as labourers, inasmuch as they failed to give any detail of the projects they were working on.

In this respect, reliance is placed on the case of “Muhammad Rafiq v. State”(2014 SCMR 1698) wherein the august Supreme Court of Pakistan rejected the claim of witnesses who lived one kilometre away from the occurrence, but on the day of occurrence stated to be present near the spot as they working as labourers, inasmuch as they failed to give any detail of the projects they were working on. Reliance is also placed on the case of “Usman alias Kaloo v. State” (2017 SCMR 622) wherein the august Supreme Court of Pakistan held that the ocular account of the incident had been furnished by Zahoor Ahmad complainant, Ghulam Farid, and Manzoor Ahmed, who were all residents of some other houses and they were not inmates of the house wherein the occurrence had taken place and the said eye-witnesses were, thus, chance witnesses and not worthy of reliance.Reliance is also placed on the case of “Nasrullah alias Nasro v. The State”(2017 SCMR 724) wherein the august Supreme Court of Pakistan observed as under:- “In the case in hand the eye-witnesses produced by the prosecution lived eighty kilometers away from the scene of the crime, their stated reason for presence in the house of occurrence at the time of incident in issue had never been established through any independent evidence.” We have also marked with concern that the mouth and eyes of the deceased were found open at the time of preparation of the inquest report (Exh.PM) by Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11), who according to his own self had arrived at the place of occurrence at about 8.00 p.m when the occurrence Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 12 had taken place at about 6.30 p.m. However, the stance set up by the prosecution in the present case was that Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) were present at the time of the death of the deceased and had remained with the dead body. The mouth and eyes of the deceased were found open at the time of preparation of the inquest report (Exh.PM), thus, if the witnesses were present then, at least after the death, as is a consistent practice of such close relatives, they would have closed the mouth and eyes of the deceased on his expiry.

However, the stance set up by the prosecution in the present case was that Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) were present at the time of the death of the deceased and had remained with the dead body. The mouth and eyes of the deceased were found open at the time of preparation of the inquest report (Exh.PM), thus, if the witnesses were present then, at least after the death, as is a consistent practice of such close relatives, they would have closed the mouth and eyes of the deceased on his expiry. Thus, the open mouth and eyes of the deceased at the time of the preparation of the inquest report (Exh.PM) by Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11) forces a hostile interpretation against the prosecution's version regarding the presence of the witnesses at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence. This fact by itself indicates that none of the witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) was present with the deceased at the time his death or even till the arrival of Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11),the Investigating Officer of the case, at the place of occurrence. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of “MUHAMMAD RAFIQUE alias FEEQA vs. The State”(2019 SCMR 1068) has held as under: “What has further irked this Court is that in column No. 9 of the Marg Report (Ex.PW9/1), and even in the Post Mortem Report (Ex.PW-10/A), the mouth of the deceased has been stated to be open, which clearly indicates that the dead body was not attended to by his close relatives after being pronounced dead. However, the stance set up by the prosecution in the present case is that Arshad Ali - the brother, and Nazir Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 13 Ahmad - the uncle of the deceased Muhammad Azam were present at the time of his death, and remained with him, even thereafter. Thus, the said posture of the deceased raises an adverse inference against the prosecution's version regarding the presence of the said persons at the place and time of occurrence.” 14. Another aspect of the case noted by us with some gravity is the fact that all the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) admitted that the occurrence had taken place inside a room of the rented house of the deceased namely Gulzar Ahmad at around 6.30 p.m and according to all the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) it was dark at the time of occurrence, still none of the witnesses stated in their statements before the learned trial court that any source of light was present and lit at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence, which allowed the witnesses to observe the occurrence.

Thus, the said posture of the deceased raises an adverse inference against the prosecution's version regarding the presence of the said persons at the place and time of occurrence.” 14. Another aspect of the case noted by us with some gravity is the fact that all the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) admitted that the occurrence had taken place inside a room of the rented house of the deceased namely Gulzar Ahmad at around 6.30 p.m and according to all the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) it was dark at the time of occurrence, still none of the witnesses stated in their statements before the learned trial court that any source of light was present and lit at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence, which allowed the witnesses to observe the occurrence. Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) during cross- examination admitted as under:- “ It is correct that usually sun sets in January at about 05/05:15 PM and it is darken at 6.30 PM.”(emphasis supplied) Similarly, Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) during his cross-examination stated as under:- “ At the time of occurrence at 6.30 PM there was darkness.”(emphasis supplied) Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) also admitted during cross-examination stated as under:- Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 14 “At the time of occurrence it was darkness.”(emphasis supplied) During the course of investigation and even before the learned trial court the no source of light was ever produced in which the three witnesses had witnessed the occurrence. Moreover, all the three prosecution witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) candidly admitted that it was dark at the time of occurrence, however, made no effort to state that they had any source of light with them or that any source of light was available at the place of occurrence in the light of which they had seen the occurrence. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General has submitted that the witnesses had a motorcycle on which they arrived at the place of occurrence, hence the light of the same could have been used to witness the occurrence. The contention of the learned Deputy Prosecutor General has no force for the reason that it was not even claimed by the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) that they had used the light of the motorcycle to witness the occurrence.

The learned Deputy Prosecutor General has submitted that the witnesses had a motorcycle on which they arrived at the place of occurrence, hence the light of the same could have been used to witness the occurrence. The contention of the learned Deputy Prosecutor General has no force for the reason that it was not even claimed by the three witnesses namely Muhammad Irshad (PW-1), Muhammad Tariq Sultan (PW-2) and Ghulam Dastageer (PW-3) that they had used the light of the motorcycle to witness the occurrence. Moreover, as mentioned above, the said motorcycle was never produced before either of the Investigating Officers of the case or before the learned trial court. Additionally, according to the the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PG) as prepared by Muhammad Jamil, Draftsman (PW-7) and the rough site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PP) as prepared by Muhammad Akbar Inspector (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, the occurrence had taken place inside a room which room was at a distance of as many as about 24 feet from the main gate of the house where the occurrence took place and in that manner the light of the motorcycle could Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 15 not have illuminated the inside of the room where the occurrence took place. According to the statement of Muhammad Irshad (PW-1) he was at a distance of 15 feet away when he had seen the occurrence and in that manner there did not exist any possibility that the light of the motorcycle would have allowed the witnesses to observe the occurrence. Moreover, Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, also admitted the absence of any light source at the place of occurrence. Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11) during cross-examination admitted as under:- “ It is correct that I did not mention any source of light Exh.PP. It is correct that at the time of occurrence i.e on 23.01.2018 at about 6.30 p.m. it was darkness.” Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, visited the place of occurrence after the occurrence but no source of light was taken into possession at the spot by the Investigating Officer alongwith other recoveries though there was no occasion for the said source of light not to have been present at the place of occurrence or being not taken into possession by the Investigating Officer during his visit at the place of occurrence, if the same was available.

Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11) during cross-examination admitted as under:- “ It is correct that I did not mention any source of light Exh.PP. It is correct that at the time of occurrence i.e on 23.01.2018 at about 6.30 p.m. it was darkness.” Muhammad Akbar, Inspector (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, visited the place of occurrence after the occurrence but no source of light was taken into possession at the spot by the Investigating Officer alongwith other recoveries though there was no occasion for the said source of light not to have been present at the place of occurrence or being not taken into possession by the Investigating Officer during his visit at the place of occurrence, if the same was available. The non-production of any light source lit at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence and the failure of the complainant of the case as well as the Investigating Officer of the case to produce the same before the learned trial court leads to only one conclusion and that being that no such source of light was available with the witnesses or available at the place of occurrence which could have enabled the eye witnesses to have identified the assailant and also spectate the role of Murder Reference No.03 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 53 of 2019 16 the assailant as acted by him during the occurrence. The prosecution witnesses failed to establish the fact of availability of any light source at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence and in absence of their ability to do so, we cannot presume the existence of such a light source. The absence of any light source has put the whole prosecution case in murk. It was admitted by the witnesses themselves that it was a dark night and as the prosecution witnesses failed to prove the availability of any light source, their statements with regard to them identifying the assailant cannot be relied upon. The failure of the prosecution witnesses to prove the presence of any light source at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence has repercussions, entailing the failure of the prosecution case. Reliance is placed on the case of “Gulfam and another v. Before proceeding any further, it would be advantageous to mention here that rigor mortis is a term which stands for the stiffness of voluntary and involuntary muscles in human body after death. It starts within 2 to 4 hours of death and fully develops in about 12-hours. Similarly, the reverse process with which rigor mortis disappears is called algor mortis. In support of duration required to develop rigor mortis, an extract from “The Principles And Practice Of Medical Jurisprudence” by Alfred Swaine Taylor,MD, is being referred hereunder:- "In sudden natural deaths occurring in a temperate climate during average seasonal conditions rigor mortis usually commences within 2 to 4 hours of death.

Thus, once there is suspicion regarding the death of a person, the following essential steps follow: firstly, there is a complete chain of police custody of the dead body, right from the moment it is taken into custody until it is handed over to the relatives, or in case they are unknown, then till his burial; secondly, post mortem examination of a dead person cannot be carried out without the authorization of competent police officer or the magistrate; thirdly, post mortem of a deceased person can only be carried out by a notified government Medical Officer; and finally, at the time of handing over the dead body by the police to the Medical Officer, all reports prepared by the investigating officer are also to be handed over to the said medical officer to assist his examination of the dead body. 11. The plea of the learned Deputy Prosecutor General that because the complainant party was having no enmity to falsely implicate the appellant in such a heinous crime thus, the evidence adduced should be believed, is entirely a misconceived one. It is a cardinal principle of justice and law that only the intrinsic worth and probative value of the evidence would play a decisive role in determining the guilt or innocence of an accused person. Even evidence of uninterested witness, not inimical to the accused, may be corrupted deliberately while evidence of inimical witness, if found consistent with the other evidence corroborating it, may be relied upon. Reliance in this regard may be placed on the case of Waqar Zaheer vs. The State (1991 PSC 281).It is a known and settled principle of law that prosecution primarily is bound to establish guilt against the accused without a shadow of reasonable doubt by producing trustworthy, convincing and coherent evidence enabling the Court to draw a conclusion whether the prosecution has succeeded in establishing accusation against the accused or otherwise and if it comes to the conclusion that charges, so imputed against the accused, have not been proved beyond a reasonable doubt, then the accused becomes entitled to acquittal.

The Crown (PLD 1953 FC 93) and Muhammad Luqman v. The State (PLD 1970 SC 10). In the instant case we have scanned the prosecution evidence in-depth and we are persuaded to hold that the prosecution has failed to produce trustworthy, confidence- inspiring and consistent evidence against the appellant. Conversely, the evidence so brought on record appears to have been fabricated to prove the prosecution case. Even otherwise the prosecution evidence is inconsistent and flawed, thus, on the basis of the same, appellant cannot further be immured because he has every right to claim guarantee of the Constitution which provides that every citizen of the country shall be dealt with in accordance with the law. 22. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of Muhammad Mansha Vs. The State(2018 SCMR 772) has enunciated the following principle: “Needless to mention that while giving the benefit of doubt to an accused it is not necessary that there should be many circumstances creating doubt. If there is a circumstance which creates reasonable doubt in a prudent mind about the guilt of the accused, then the accused would be entitled to the benefit of such doubt, not as a matter of grace and concession, but as a matter of right. It is based on the maxim, "it is better that ten guilty persons be acquitted rather than one innocent person be convicted". Reliance in this behalf can be made upon the cases of Tariq Pervez v.
